text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to address adhesions from a previous ventral hernia repair, carefully dissecting tissue stuck to the mesh. A trocar is placed in the left upper quadrant for the camera and another in the epigastric region for scissors. Once the adhesions are cleared, the small bowel is moved aside to begin medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon performs a high ligation of the vasculature, isolating the superior hemorrhoidal artery distal to the left colic artery using a vessel sealer. The medial to lateral dissection continues towards the left sidewall, with a sponge placed to protect the ureter. Lateral dissection is initiated to connect with the previously dissected medial space.

The surgeon uses a sponge to confirm the correct dissection space along the left sidewall, addressing adhesions between the sigmoid colon and bladder. The suction device is employed as a dissector to approach the fistula from above. The cystoscopy reveals a more than one-centimeter defect with solid stool passing into the bladder, indicating the need for bladder repair. Initially, the surgeon encounters a stool-filled cavity between the colon and bladder, with the bladder defect visible in the distance.

The surgeon separates the colon from the back of the bladder, exposing the fistula deep within a stool-filled cavity. The colon is lengthened beneath the fistula to prepare for stapling. The surgeon cleans the cavity and takes the proximal mesentery, while considering debridement of the friable bladder wall to healthier tissue for suturing. The rectum is cleaned, leaving a longer Hartmann stump to prevent retraction into the pelvis.

The surgeon marks a centimeter around the hole in the bladder to resect friable tissue and expose healthier bladder wall. The incision goes full thickness into the bladder, revealing signs of bullous emphysema associated with bladder fistulas. The surgeon uses an absorbable suture to repair the muscle wall and mucosa.

The surgeon performs a two-layer suture repair on the bladder, initially opposing the mucosa to mucosa and then imbricating the first suture line with a second suture. The tissue, previously exposed to a stool-filled cavity, is carefully pulled together, with the sutures holding well and the repair coming together nicely.
The surgeon tests the bladder with methylene blue, observing no leakage from the bladder. The edges of the inflammatory rind have been debrided, and the bladder appears intact.
